mnemonics.title: CAP Parts Remember
mnemonics.content: CAP = Can't All Provide - You can't have all three, pick any two!

mnemonics.title: CP vs AP Decision
mnemonics.content:
CP = Consistency over Availability (choose when data accuracy is critical like banking)
AP = Availability over Consistency (choose when uptime matters like social media)

acronyms.usage: Fundamental theorem in distributed databases by Eric Brewer

acronyms.expansion: Partition, Availability, Consistency, Else, Latency, Consistency
acronyms.usage: Extension of CAP theorem adding latency vs consistency trade-off
Properties of database transactions, relates to strong consistency

acronyms.expansion: Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency
acronyms.usage: Alternative to ACID for distributed systems, used by AP databases

visualTips.visualization: Draw a triangle with Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance at corners. Show that you can only pick TWO at a time. During partitions, you must move along an edge to choose between C and A.
Start: Is partition occurring? Yes → Choose CP (consistency) or AP (availability). No → Consider latency vs consistency trade-off (PACELC).
Place databases on CAP diagram: Cassandra (AP corner), MongoDB (CP near middle), MySQL (CA in normal, CP during partitions).

analogies.analogy: Like a group project where team members in different rooms can't communicate - they must either wait for everyone to agree (consistency) or work independently and sync later (availability).
Banking ATM: During network issues, ATM may reject transactions (CP - consistency prioritized) OR allow withdrawals but risk overdraw (AP - availability prioritized).
Like Wikipedia - when you edit, changes propagate to all servers over time. You might see slightly old data briefly, but eventually all see the same content.
